policy_statement: Expand parking policies to incorporate goals beyond customer access, including using new revenue to support transit, bicycle, and pedestrian infrastructure and services by 2030 (state legislative action required).
mechanism_description: The city plans to expand parking policies so new parking revenue can support transit, bicycle, and pedestrian infrastructure and services, encouraging travel by lower-emission modes and reducing transportation emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Parking price signals + reinvestment in low-carbon modes → modal shift / ↓ car VKT → ↓ fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: The city increases/expands paid parking (rates, zones, time limits, set) and earmarks incremental parking revenue to improve transit service and walking/biking networks.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Higher parking costs and better alternatives shift travel behavior away from single-occupancy vehicles (mode shift, trip consolidation, reduced VKT), lowering gasoline/diesel consumption.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced transportation-sector CO₂ (and some co-emitted pollutants), with potential additional reductions from reduced congestion/idling depending on implementation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced on-road fossil-fuel combustion from fewer/shorter car trips due to mode shift and demand reduction induced by parking pricing and reinvestment.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Pricing and reinvestment change travel choices, leading to lower car VKT and fuel combustion, which directly reduces GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although the policy reallocates/uses revenue (a fiscal/resource allocation feature), the dominant climate effect is emissions mitigation from transport behavior change, not primarily “resource efficiency” in the sense of same service with less energy input, nor adaptation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If parking revenues were used mainly for non-transport purposes, the mitigation effect could weaken; if reinvestment focuses on electrification, mitigation remains but via a different sub-pathway. Congestion effects are secondary relative to VKT/fuel reduction.  
4) Confirm applicability to ALL instances: Any policy with this mechanism—parking pricing coupled with reinvestment into transit/active modes to induce mode shift—has its main climate impact through reducing fossil-fuel vehicle travel and thus emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the policy is best tagged as pricing, demand management, transit investment, active mobility, equity-focused earmarking, etc. depends on policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level coding.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If parking pricing increases circling/cruising due to insufficient supply management or poor wayfinding, short-term emissions could rise locally, but the mechanism’s intended and typical dominant effect remains reduced driving demand and emissions. If revenues fund general budgets or roadway expansion, the mechanism would no longer match this canonical pathway.
key_indicators: "Expand parking policies"; "new revenue"; "allowing spending of new revenue to support improvements in transit, bicycle, and pedestrian infrastructure and services"; "Actions to Implement by 2030"
co_benefits: Reduced congestion and idling; improved air quality; traffic safety benefits; increased accessibility and public health from active transportation.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a pricing mechanism if the policy explicitly specified parking pricing/fees (it implies revenue generation but does not state pricing instruments). It also contains an earmarking/reinvestment element that makes it more than pricing alone.
